Coexpression
 
and
 
Immune
 
Interactions
 
of

Conjugate
 
Targets
 
in

Cancer

High-grade
 
serous
 
ovarian
 
carcinoma
 
(HGSC)
 
remains
 
in
 
need
 
of
 
new
 
targeted
 
therapies,
 
particularly
 
for
 
patients
 
who
 
are
 
ineligible
 
for
 
PARP
 
inhibitors
 
(PARPi)
 
or
 
whose
 
disease
 
progresses
 
despite
 
them.
 
Antibody-drug
 
conjugates
 
(ADCs)
 
may
 
provide
 
an
 
alternative,
 
but
 
their
 
clinical
 
impact
 
depends
 
on
 
more
 
than
 
target
 
abundance
 
alone.
 
Traditional
 
bulk
 
assays
 
fail
 
to
 
capture
 
the
 
spatial
 
distribution
 
of
 
target-positive
 
cells
 
and
 
their

on
 
more
 
than
 
target
 
abundance
 
alone.
 
Traditional
 
bulk
 
assays
 
fail
 
to
 
capture
 
the
 
spatial
 
distribution
 
of
 
target-positive
 
cells
 
and
 
their
 
interactions
 
with
 
the
 
immune
 
cells.
 
To
 
overcome
 
this
 
limitation,
 
we
 
aim
 
to
 
resolve
 
the
 
in
 
situ
 
protein
 
and
 
transcriptomic
 
architecture
 
to
 
identify
 
treatment-response
 
delineators
 
and
 
improve
 
patient
 
stratification
 
for
 
ADC-therapy.
 
Formalin-fixed,
 
paraffin-embedded
 
(FFPE)
 
samples
 
from
 
the
 
prospective
 
Oncosys-OVA
 
trial
 
(NCT06117384)
 
were
 
used
 
to
 
implement
 
an
 
integrated
 
multi-omic
 
framework
 
quantifying
 
the
 
spatial
 
and
 
transcriptomic
 
landscapes
 
of
 
ADC
 
targets.
 
Multiplexed
 
tissue
 
cyclic
 
immunofluorescence
 
(tCycIF)
 
was
 
used
 
to
 
track
 
the
 
expression
 
of
 
three
 
ADC
 
targets
 
(FOLR1,
 
TROP2,
 
and
 
B7-H4
 
)
 
alongside
 
tumour,
 
stromal,
 
and
 
immune-lineage
 
markers.
 
To
 
understand
 
the
 
observed
 
spatial
 
composition,
 
we
 
combined
 
bulk
 
RNA
 
sequencing,
 
whole-exome
 
sequencing,
 
spatial
 
transcriptomics
 
via
 
GeoMx,
 
and
 
single-cell
 
spatial
 
transcriptomics
 
with
 
Xenium.
 
Bulk
 
RNA-seq
 
deconvolution
 
analysis
 
of
 
301
 
samples
 
showed
 
that
 
ADC
 
targets
 
are
 
highly
 
tumour-enriched
 
in
 
tumour
 
cells
 
and

cs
 
with
 
Xenium.
 
Bulk
 
RNA-seq
 
deconvolution
 
analysis
 
of
 
301
 
samples
 
showed
 
that
 
ADC
 
targets
 
are
 
highly
 
tumour-enriched
 
in
 
tumour
 
cells
 
and
 
expressed
 
at
 
low
 
levels
 
in
 
immune
 
and
 
stromal
 
compartments.
 
Co-expression
 
analysis
 
of
 
FOLR1,
 
TROP2,
 
and
 
B7-H4
 
showed
 
only
 
weak,
 
non-significant
 
correlations
 
(p
 
=
 
-0.16
 
to
 
0.21),
 
supporting
 
largely
 
independent
 
expression
 
patterns.
 
Across
 
498
 
tumour
 
regions
 
from
 
53
 
samples
 
profiled
 
by
 
GeoMx,
 
spatial
 
transcriptomics
 
revealed
 
both
 
intra-
 
and
 
inter-patient
 
diversity,
 
with
 
inter-patient
 
variation
 
predominating.
 
We
 
profiled
 
109
 
samples
 
at
 
single-cell
 
resolution,
 
and
 
we
 
successfully
 
mapped
 
12
 
distinct
 
cell
 
populations
 
across
 
the
 
tumour
 
microenvironment:
 
cancer
 
cells,
 
NK
 
cells,
 
Treg
 
cells,
 
B
 
cells,
 
CD8+,
 
CD4+,
 
and
 
CD3+
 
T
 
cells,
 
macrophages,
 
dendritic
 
cells,
 
endothelial
 
cells,
 
and
 
cancer-associated
 
fibroblasts
 
(CAFs).
 
Initial
 
tCycIF
 
analysis
 
revealed
 
heterogeneous
 
expression,
 
indicating
 
multiple
 
regions
 
with
 
distinct
 
ADC-target
 
expressions
 
within
 
a
 
sample.
 
We
 
identified
 
regions
 
with
 
overlapping,
 
mutually
 
exclusive,
 
and
 
non-detectable
 
target
 
expression.

Despite
 
ADCs’
 
tumour
 
specificity,
 
their
 
pronounced
 
spatial
 
heterogeneity—both
 
between
 
and
 
within
 
patients—suggests
 
that
 
single-agent
 
therapies
 
are
 
unlikely
 
to
 
suffice
 
for
 
all
 
tumour
 
niches.
 
By
 
integrating
 
spatial
 
proteomics,
 
transcriptomics,
 
and
 
clinical
 
data,
 
we
 
lay
 
the
 
groundwork
 
for
 
rational
 
patient
 
stratification
 
for
 
ADC-therapies
 
in
 
HGSC.
 
 
 
 
Keywords:
 
ADC-targets,
 
ovarian
 
cancer ,
 
multiomics,
 
spatial
 
proteomics ,
 
multiomics
 
integration
